title: Warsh Era Opens Hawkish, China Hits Back on Trade, Oil Spikes on Iran
subhead: Kevin Warsh's first Fed meeting signals rate hikes ahead with half the FOMC leaning tighter, China retaliates against US blacklist with rare earth export controls, and Brent jumps past $82 as Trump threatens military action against Iran.

sections.heading: Warsh's Fed: No Guidance, All Hawkish
sections.body:
Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh presided over his first FOMC meeting on June 17, keeping the federal funds rate at 3.50%-3.75% — but the real signal was who voted for what comes next. Nine of 18 FOMC members now project at least one rate hike before year-end, versus eight who favor holding steady and one who sees room to cut, according to USA Today's reading of the dot plot. Warsh himself broke precedent by declining to submit his own rate projection.

The meeting statement was nearly half the length of the April version, and Warsh refused to offer forward guidance on the rate path at his press conference, saying only that the Fed would deliver 'price stability.' He announced five new task forces covering Fed communication, balance sheet, data sources, inflation framework, and productivity and jobs — staffed by both insiders and outsiders, with recommendations due this fall.

Markets repriced quickly: the Nasdaq sold off and the 10-year Treasury yield climbed to 4.45%, up 10-15 basis points in the immediate aftermath, according to The Bull. Christian Hoffmann of Thornburg Investment Management called the task forces 'constructive' if genuinely aimed at improving the Fed's data and reaction function, while Gbenga Ajilore of the Center on Budget and Policy Priorities cautioned that 'a lot of times, people will create a task force to do something that they already want to do.'

President Trump, who spent years publicly pressuring former Chair Jerome Powell for rate cuts, brushed off the hold decision, telling reporters in Paris: 'It's all right. Whatever.' He called Warsh 'a good guy' but added that a hike 'just keeps the country down.' Bloomberg economists Anna Wong, Stuart Paul, and Eliza Winger noted that even though Warsh didn't submit his own dot, 'his tone at the news conference seemed notably hawkish to us. A hot PCE inflation reading will likely reinforce that hawkish message.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Warsh era marks a deliberate break from the Powell playbook: less transparency, more optionality, and a committee tilting toward tightening. For markets priced for cuts, the pivot to hike-talk is the story.

sections.heading: PCE Inflation Report Looms as Test of Hawkish Case
sections.body:
The Fed's preferred inflation gauge — the PCE price index for May — lands this Thursday, and forecasters expect it to show acceleration on both a monthly and year-over-year basis, according to Bloomberg coverage cited by The Star (Malaysia). The data will be the first major inflation read since Warsh's debut meeting and will either validate or challenge the growing consensus on the FOMC for rate hikes.

The PCE report caps what Bloomberg described as 'a month of bad inflation data,' with prior releases showing the energy shock from the Iran conflict spreading across the broader economy. Key Fed speakers this week include New York Fed President John Williams on June 25, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee the same day, and Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari on June 26. With Warsh withholding his own projections, their commentary will carry outsized weight as markets try to read the new regime's reaction function.

Also on the US data calendar: new home sales (Wednesday), durable goods orders (Thursday), international merchandise trade (Friday), and the University of Michigan consumer sentiment final for June (Friday). Canada releases CPI Monday, expected to show acceleration to 3% on gasoline prices.
sections.whyItMatters: A hot PCE print gives the hawkish half of the FOMC ammunition to move on rates. With Warsh refusing to telegraph his views, every data release becomes a potential trigger.

sections.heading: China Retaliates: Rare Earth Export Controls and Procurement Bans
sections.body:
China escalated the trade conflict with the United States on Monday, imposing export controls on 10 US entities and barring 46 American companies from Chinese government procurement, according to China's Commerce Ministry and reported by AP and Reuters. The export controls target firms including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth — the two American rare earth producers — along with defense contractors and drone makers such as Red Cat Holdings, Teal Drones, L3Harris Maritime Services, Oshkosh Defense, and Ball Aerospace. Chinese companies are now prohibited from supplying 'dual-use' items to these firms, and third-country actors are also banned from transferring China-origin dual-use goods to them.

The procurement ban covers 46 US companies including Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, and Boeing's defense division, though companies operating in China through locally invested entities are exempt, according to Newsbytes. China's Commerce Ministry called the measures retaliation for the Pentagon's expansion of its 'Chinese Military Companies' list, which recently added firms including Alibaba, Baidu, Tencent, CATL, DJI, Huawei, and Hikvision. Baidu called the designation 'totally baseless.'

The move follows the Trump-Xi meeting in May and runs counter to the consensus both leaders reportedly reached, according to China's own prior statements. The rare earth dimension is especially pointed: MP Materials operates the only active rare earth mine in the United States, and China controls roughly 70% of global rare earth processing capacity. Targeting US rare earth firms signals Beijing's willingness to weaponize its dominance in critical mineral supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the sharpest Sino-American trade escalation since the Trump-Xi meeting in May. By hitting rare earth producers, China is demonstrating it can inflict pain far beyond tariffs — directly on the Pentagon's critical minerals supply chain.

sections.heading: Oil Surges as Trump Threatens Iran, Hormuz Fears Return
sections.body:
Brent crude briefly hit $82.30 a barrel and WTI moved above $78 on Monday, up roughly 2.2%, after President Trump warned of possible military action against Iran if Hezbollah continued attacks on Israel, according to Goodreturns. The comments raised doubts over whether the US-Iran diplomatic effort — currently underway at Bürgenstock in Switzerland within a 60-day negotiation window — can hold. Iranian media reported that Tehran had suspended talks after Trump's warning, though sources said negotiations continued despite a tense opening.

The Strait of Hormuz remains the focal risk. Iran again claimed to have shut the waterway, but oil shipments continued largely as normal over the weekend, limiting the scale of the surge. Trump separately told Fox News he had warned Iranian leaders that any attempt to close Hormuz would carry 'severe consequences.' Ponmudi R, CEO of Enrich Money, said the uncertainty had pushed crude prices modestly higher and could weigh on sentiment near-term.

For India — the world's third-largest crude importer — the stakes are acute. Every $1/barrel increase adds roughly $2.1 billion to India's annual import bill, according to Financial Express. India's refiners have already pivoted hard toward Russian crude, with June imports from Russia hitting a record 2.6 million barrels per day, accounting for 53.5% of total Indian crude imports, per Kpler vessel-tracking data. That surpasses the previous record of 2.2 million bpd set in May 2023.

The surge was driven by the Hormuz disruption: Iraqi exports collapsed because Iraq relies almost entirely on the strait, while Saudi Arabia and the UAE maintained partial flows through pipeline bypass routes. The US sanctions waiver for Russian oil expired this week, and it remains unclear whether Washington will renew it. Analyst Sumit Ritolia of Kpler said Russian barrels remain competitive and 'we expect India's imports of Russian crude to remain robust, even if not at record-high levels.' Energy analyst Natalia Katona told the Indian Express that Russian crude is now 'increasingly treated as a reliable base-load supply source rather than an opportunistic discounted barrel.'
sections.whyItMatters: Oil is repricing geopolitical risk, not physical shortage — flows through Hormuz continue. But India's structural pivot to Russian crude is now quantifiable at over half of total imports, and the expired US waiver creates a policy gap that could tighten if Moscow's discounts erode.

sections.heading: AI Capex Meets Rising Rates: The Hyperscaler Squeeze
sections.body:
The Fed's hawkish shift landed directly on the most leveraged sector in global markets. Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Meta are projected to deploy a combined $750 billion in capital expenditures this year, an increase exceeding 80% from 2025, according to The Bull citing Goldman Sachs. The bank warned the true figure could approach $920 billion, noting that analyst estimates have proven 'too conservative' for three consecutive years.

Capex as a percentage of operating cash flow for the hyperscalers now approaches 90-100%, versus a historical norm around 40%, Goldman Sachs data shows. Amazon is the most extreme case: with roughly $200 billion in 2026 capital spending, multiple sell-side forecasts expect the company to post negative free cash flow of $17-28 billion, its first such year in modern memory. Microsoft and Alphabet have each guided to $175-190 billion in capex, while Meta plans $80-100 billion.

To fund the gap, tech giants have borrowed over $100 billion in recent bond offerings explicitly tied to AI buildouts, with projections of $1.0-1.5 trillion in new debt over coming years if current trajectories hold. Alphabet has even announced equity raises. When the FOMC signals that half its members see a rate hike before year-end, it directly raises the discount rate on trillions of dollars in expected future AI profits — and the cost of the debt financing those bets.

The Nasdaq sold off on the Fed meeting, and Amazon shares came under particular scrutiny over its negative FCF trajectory. Even Nvidia saw valuation compression despite its dominance in AI infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: The hyperscaler capex boom was always a bet on cheap capital. Warsh's Fed just made that bet more expensive. The collision between $750B in AI spending and a central bank signaling higher rates is the defining valuation tension of 2026.

sections.heading: Asia Week Ahead: BoJ Speaks, Singapore Inflation, Australia Trimmed Mean
sections.body:
Asian markets face a crowded calendar this week, with central bank communication taking center stage. Bank of Japan Deputy Governor Ryozo Himino speaks Wednesday, the same day the BoJ releases its summary of opinions from the June meeting where it raised rates to their highest level in 31 years, according to The Star. Board member Naoki Tamura speaks Thursday. Japan's flash PMIs will be watched for whether the recovery is broadening beyond exports.

Singapore releases headline inflation data Tuesday, expected to accelerate to 2% year-over-year in May. The Monetary Authority of Singapore's tightening stance — which has involved allowing the Singapore dollar to appreciate — will be tested if price pressures prove stickier than expected. The Strait Times reported that the MAS's currency-centered approach contrasts with the interest-rate-based frameworks of most major central banks, giving it a different transmission mechanism for managing imported inflation.

Australia's trimmed mean CPI for May is due Wednesday after quickening to 3.4% in the prior month. RBA official Andrew Hauser speaks hours after the data release. India's HSBC PMIs will show whether the region's standout growth story is still accelerating or settling at a strong pace. Thailand's central bank makes a policy decision Wednesday, balancing weak domestic demand against currency and external pressures.

In China, the Loan Prime Rate fixing on Monday is expected to be unchanged, but remains an important signal given uneven demand and doubts over the recovery. South Korea's early export data for June showed continued strength, driven by the semiconductor and AI boom, according to the Singapore Business Times.
sections.whyItMatters: Asia's central banks are on divergent paths: the BoJ hiking, the MAS tightening through its currency, the RBA watching inflation creep up, and Thailand stuck between weak demand and external pressure. How they respond to the Fed's hawkish shift will determine whether global rate repricing stays orderly or spirals.

bottomLine: Kevin Warsh's debut as Fed chair delivered a clear message: less guidance, more hawkish optionality, with nine FOMC members now seeing a rate hike before year-end. Thursday's PCE data will be the first test. Meanwhile, China escalated the trade war by hitting US rare earth producers and defense firms with export controls — a move that goes beyond tariffs into critical mineral supply chain weaponization. Oil spiked on Trump's Iran threats, but Hormuz remains open; the bigger structural story is India's record pivot to Russian crude at 53.5% of imports. And the Fed's hawkish turn collides directly with tech's $750B AI capex binge, making the cost of capital the defining tension of the quarter. Asian central banks now face their own divergence test: BoJ hiking, MAS tightening via currency, and Thailand stuck between weak demand and external pressure.
sources: USA Today, Bloomberg via The Star Malaysia, Goodreturns India, Financial Express India, Kpler, AP News, Reuters, Newsbytes, The Bull Australia, Morningstar, Singapore Business Times, Strait Times, Indian Express, Fox News
